hints.text: Plates: blue strips both sides OR a **single red strip on the left** — the only European country with red on the left (Italy = blue both but shorter front)
All Gen4 = **smallcam**; Gen3 often has **rifts** (poor stitching; equally common in Montenegro)
Bollards = **black top, red+grey reflector, white bottom** (Italy uses the same)
Poletops with an **upside-down triangle** shape (also RS/CZ/SK/RO) + simpler horizontal-bar poles
Chevrons **white on black** (Italy/Greece/Spain; Montenegro's differ); sign backs often painted black
Distinct **S-shape on stop signs** (short, angled ends — like Italy); bold speed-sign font (Montenegro's is thinner)
**Albanian** language: letters **Ë ë** (& Ç ç); street = **rruga**
Mountainous & Mediterranean; **exposed red soil** roadside; **rooftop cylinder water tanks**; Italian-style pastel buildings; **Mercedes** everywhere
Gen3 = long-antenna + rifts, or short-antenna + no rifts; lowlands in the west (Tirana/Shkodër/Vlorë) + the flat Korçë plateau SE; highest mountains far north & south
Plate has an **orange dot on the left** — unique in Europe to Andorra
Only **Gen2 + Gen3**; Gen3 car has a medium-short antenna & was all shot in **October → autumn colours**
Lots of **stone buildings** everywhere (also just over the border in the French/Spanish Pyrenees)
**Catalan** (street = **carrer**; uses *i* not *y* for 'and'); wooden snow poles
km markers use **CG / CS** road numbers
Guardrails w/ **yellow reflectors** (in Europe only AD/ES/BE); bollard w/ an orange reflector (also Spain)
Pedestrian sign **8 stripes** (only Spain also has 8); yellow & turquoise-green traffic cones countrywide
Mountainous; **Andorra la Vella** = highest European capital (Caldea tower + sky wheel); **Pas de la Casa** = treeless high ski town
Plate: black w/ **black dot** in middle (**AR only**), or white w/ blue top bar (blue bar also Brazil)
Gen3 **black car** (S. America: only AR/PE/UY); smallcam exists (AR/PE/BR)
**White-and-red chevrons** (only country in the Americas with these)
Round concrete poles (3 lines, alternating) + doubled poles; **wooden A-frame poles** (only AR in S. America)
km markers white w/ black top strip: **RN** = national, **RP** = provincial road
Default vibe: flat, treeless, sparsely populated, "wide open" & desolate
Far **west** mountainous (Andes); east rarely has mountains
**Red soil → Misiones** (Brazil-like); Chaco/Formosa = sandy soil + low bushes
**Cardón cactus** (candelabra) → NW mountains (Salta, Jujuy, Catamarca, Tucumán)
Tierra del Fuego: flat grass + beech forest; Neuquén = flat w/ single poplar rows
Pampas grass → Pampas; sugarcane → Tucumán
Vineyards + mountains to the west + flat east → Mendoza wine region
Salt pans → Jujuy; red bricks (Bolivia-like) → NW (western Jujuy)
**Eucalyptus (gum) trees** everywhere, light bark; drives on the **left**
Bollard: white, **red reflector front / grey back** (NZ = red wraps around the back)
Almost all **white** road lines — yellow lines → South Africa or NZ instead
Give-way sign: white border + **black** text (NZ = red text); speed sign red circle on white rectangle (NZ = no rectangle)
Gen3 = long antenna; chevrons white-on-black & black-on-yellow
**Stobie pole** (two steel joists + concrete slab middle) is iconic to South Australia
Rusty metal poles w/ holes; **130 km/h** speed limit = NT only; termite mounds
**Yellow signposts**; black-and-white **stripy bollards** unique to WA; green-based poles
~**64% of concrete poles** are in Victoria; 'C' roads only in Victoria & Tasmania
Unique **pole-top coils** angled upward; fluorescent-border road signs; black-and-yellow bollard
**Red 'No Stopping' signs** (other states white); snorkel-fitted rare cars → NSW/ACT
Interior = **red soil**, dry; SE + SW corners greener; can "fry eggs" red outback
Tasmania: cool temperate green rolling hills (like NZ/S. Chile)
Coastal N. Queensland tropical: palms + sugarcane; Darwin (NT) dark red soil + black tree trunks
Different states use different **licence-plate** designs + slogans; 2-digit phone area codes map to state
Plate long white + EU blue strip (Italy = 2 strips & shorter; Swiss = no strip)
Bollards white w/ a **black top**, dark-red or grey reflector, small **nipple cap** (Slovenia/Montenegro = bright red reflector)
Chevrons **white-on-red or red-on-yellow** (Germany mostly red-on-white)
**Round wooden poles** are the most common pole type
German (**Ä Ö Ü ß**); camera meta separates the 3: **Austria = Gen3 + Gen4**, Germany = Gen4 only, Switzerland = low-cam
Direction & town-entry signs = **white bg, blue outline, unique font** (Germany = yellow town signs)
Pedestrian sign = **two horizontal dashed lines** (not zebra stripes) — unique; all neighbours use zebra
Road markers **blue w/ white numbers + one decimal (comma)** (Slovenia white; Germany white outline)
Bus-stop signs = **yellow-green circle w/ H**; **"EINBAHN"** one-way sign (Germany = "Einbahnstraße")
Dead-end sign = white **upside-down T**; **yellow gas poles** (snow-pole-like) are unique to Austria
Alpine architecture: light walls, wooden balconies/panelling on the top floor, dark wooden roofs extending outward
**Mostly mountainous** (central/west); flatter east near borders; A/B roads number **east→west**; each state has own flag + bus company; vineyards → far east
**Vienna** = dark-blue serif street plaques (leading number = district); Vorarlberg neon-yellow bus poles; Vienna/Innsbruck flag-tape on poles
Plates white, black, or **turquoise** (the turquoise plate is unique to Bangladesh)
**Bengali** script: continuous **line along the top** of words + many **triangular** letter shapes (vs Devanagari)
Gen3 cars: red-mirror-w/-bars, classic white, **striped white** (don't confuse w/ Cambodia); Gen4 black/white; shitcam exists
**Green auto-rickshaws** very common throughout the country
**Black-and-white striped signposts** (also LK/BT/IN/NP/MY)
**Red-and-white concrete bollards** nationwide (round or square form)
Streets often **tree-lined** (white-painted trunk bottoms); water bodies + rice fields right beside roads
**Tin roofs** very common; Gen4 coverage often has a **smoky/hazy feel**
Roof east/west divide: **west = sheet-metal hip roofs**; **east (Sylhet) = metal roofs w/ a triangular 'window' below the ridge**
Mostly dead-flat; **rice** grown twice yearly (its growth stage/colour → region in Gen4); forested hills in E. Chittagong; tea → Sylhet highlands
Only coverage (bar spills) = a **single walking trekker in historic central Minsk**, mostly pedestrian-only streets
Historic Minsk = well-kept **classical European architecture**, white/pastel walls
The **Holy Spirit Cathedral** (two towers) features prominently and can be seen from afar
Signs in **Belarusian or Russian** (both Cyrillic); letters **ў, і** are not in Russian
Minsk-centre street signs are **sea-green** ("вул" = street); pavement mostly grey rectangular tiles
Plates use **red text** (recognisable even through the blur)
Bollards white w/ a **yellow rectangular reflector** (Denmark = red stripe on top); also a dark-brown bollard w/ two red stripes
A-type guardrails w/ **yellow reflectors** (in Europe only ES/AD/BE)
Poles concrete, square, w/ **tiny holes** + small metal wrappings (oval-hole variant too)
Directional signs blue/white; road signs often carry an **additional small blue sub-sign** (white in other countries)
Pedestrian sign = **two dotted lines, man with a square head** (France/Netherlands use a 5-stripe zebra)
Chevrons **white w/ red arrow** (also NL, never France); km markers often **green** (sometimes red text)
Many rural roads are **concrete slabs** (like PH/TH; NL almost never); all-white lines + **bike lanes on both sides**
**Red brick houses** (north/west); stone, grey, more French-looking houses (south)
Signposts (and sometimes sign backs) often **orange** — unique to Belgium; **gutters on both sides** of almost all roads
Bus stops = **yellow signposts** (yellow shelter roofs); small orange-diamond priority signs; red **H hydrant** signs
**Most blurred houses after Germany**; rarely a red car w/ long antenna (far more common in Ukraine)

**3 languages**: Dutch/Flemish (N), French (S/Wallonia), German (Ostbelgien E); Brussels bilingual; Flanders warning signs = thick red, no white border (= Spain); **Ardennes** SE = steep forested hills
Plates almost always **full red** (the only Asian country w/ red plates); taxis = **yellow plates + yellow roofs**
**Grey car, no antenna** — the only left-driving country with a grey Google car
**Stone-block safety barriers** on winding rural roads (a great rural-Bhutan clue); guardrails are rare
Very distinct architecture: **tin roofs + painted wooden frames**
**Dzongkha** script — unique-looking letters (found only in Bhutan on Street View)
**Mountainous** (most coverage on mountain roads); **prayer flags** by the road; flat land only in the south
Road markers = **yellow top / white bottom** (national highways show the road number on top → pinpoint the road)
City signs (red or blue) show the **town name**; **black-and-white striped signposts** (also LK/IN/NP/MY/BD)
Coverage on main roads + cities; south is flat (only 4 regions covered); **Phobjikha Valley** = the biggest open flat valley
Plates **short, white w/ blue text** (blue hard to see → often looks plain white)
Only **Gen3**, a solid **white car, no antenna** (in Latin America also Peru & Chile)
Buildings often **large red brick w/ an 'unfinished' look** (also common in parts of Peru)
Middle lines yellow / white / **mixed**, outer always white (Chile never mixes; Peru often has outer-only roads)
Concrete-slab + **cobblestone roads** (neat stone lines separating disorganised cobbles in between)
**Bulky white square signposts** (Peru uses black-and-white 'zebra' signposts)
Guardrail markings with **yellow stripes on the outside** (Peru = inverse, black outside)
Poles inconsistent (round concrete w/ pinholes + wooden); **extremely curvy wooden poles** (east) are a great Bolivia clue
km markers = **black top ('Km') / white bottom**, no road number (Argentina's have a road number)
Traditional dress (bowler hats, pollera skirts); **MAS/Evo** graffiti + party colours (black/white/blue); **Wiphala** flag; **Tigo** telecom (not in Peru)

Coverage limited to central & central-west (6 cities: La Paz/El Alto, Cochabamba, Oruro, Sucre, Potosí, Santa Cruz); **Altiplano** plateau (tufted 'hedgehog' grass), lusher north / arider south; reddish soil
**White car, no antenna** (in Africa also ZA / Lesotho / Eswatini)
Plates **white front / yellow rear** (UK-style) — only Botswana & Namibia in southern Africa predominantly have yellow rear plates
Signs in **English**; town names from indigenous languages; **Afrikaans very rare** (unlike South Africa)
Landscape **dry & mostly flat**: shrubs, small trees, golden grass; sandy soil white → red
Road lines **yellow outer / white middle** (Southern Africa; note Botswana drives **left**)
Unique **black-and-yellow striped signposts** (in S. Africa only Botswana); striped kerbs & guardrails
Green direction signs; **road numbers start with A** (South Africa never starts with A); some older **blue-background** signs (~unique to Botswana)
Houses range shack → bungalow, exposed grey brick, occasional round huts; **random small blurs** common in coverage
Coverage sparse (densest in the east + near Gaborone); mostly **pancake-flat**, hills only in ~2 areas (SE between Gaborone & Lobatse)
Plate: white + **blue top stripe** (since 2018); red = commercial
**Portuguese**: -ão (not -ón), -nh (not -ñ), -lh (not -ll)
**Red soil** across most of the country (not NE, not most of Rio Grande do Sul)
"Ladder" poles: rectangular, small holes near top (also Paraguay)
Road-sign backs painted **black** (also Paraguay)
Chevrons **yellow-on-black** (rest of S. America uses black-on-yellow)
Roads: double yellow centre + white outer lines; white painted kerbs common
Transparent satellite dishes (unique in Latin America); blue Fortlev roof water tanks
Gen3 car: white, stubby antenna w/ diagonal coil (also EC/CO/MX); smallcam exists (only BR/PE/AR in S. America)
Coverage mostly eastern half; Amazon very sparse
NE = Caatinga: dry soil, shrubs, succulents
Paraná pines (araucaria, flat-top branches) → south: PR, SC, RS
Carnaúba palms (fan leaves, spiked lower trunk) → CE, PI, RN
Area codes (DDD) grouped by 1st digit; ending in **1** = state capital
2-letter state acronyms on km markers / state road numbers
CEP postcode first 2 digits: numbering runs anti-clockwise from São Paulo → ends Rio Grande do Sul
Round concrete poles → south (esp. São Paulo); wooden poles → RS, Roraima, Amazonas, RJ
Open grassy plains, distant mountains, thin bunched grass
"Brown mushroom" insulators → only Pernambuco; "white mushroom" → Bahia
"Pencil pole" (smooth cone top) → Rio de Janeiro; pole street-names painted → Paraná
Plate standard European (blue strip left)
**Cyrillic** (Latin often too on signs); letter **Ъ** (only Bulgarian & Russian); street = улица (ul.)
Cylindrical concrete poles; poletop w/ **3 large alternating upward-hook insulators** is ~unique to Bulgaria
Chevrons **red on white** (like RO/TR/MK); directional & town signs show **two lines** (Cyrillic + Latin) — visible even from afar
Pedestrian sign = **4 stripes, person wears a hat** (no other E/Central-European sign has 4 stripes)
Bollards are **rare** & generic (red front / white back, inconsistent shape)
**Thin road lines** (Romania's are thick); generally poor road quality w/ cracks & potholes
Orange tiled roofs w/ **uneven mortar patches** (famously rough-looking); distinctive metallic trash bins
Gen3 mostly **March/April dead 'winter' look**; grey-tape **'flag antenna'**; Gen4 blue/red/black cars (red → central/SE)
**Balkan Mtns** split the country N/S (flat north to Romania); **Rhodope/Pirin/Rila** = highest peaks (S/SW); **area codes ordered by first digit**
**Nessebar/Sozopol** old towns (stone + white walls + wood, cobbled streets); Sofia = white signs w/ two red strips; Stara Zagora = brown signs w/ yellow text
Plates white w/ blue letters (may look plain white through blur); **government vehicles = green plates**
Gen3: a **short 3-ridge stubby-antenna** car, or a **white striped car** (white-left/yellow-right stripe, no antenna — unique to Cambodia); shitcam also exists
Drives **right**; **Khmer** script — very intricate & curvy (Thai/Lao look similar but less intricate)
Poles: **concrete ladder poles** + round concrete w/ small holes (Vietnam similar); Thailand's square pinhole poles are rare here
Bollards: **red-white striped stone** or **red-top/white-base w/ a reflector** — often used instead of chevrons on curves
Stone **road markers** (white base, rounded red top; road number on the narrow side, town + distance on the main side)
Chevrons **black w/ yellow arrows** (all other SE Asian countries use the reverse)
Roads: yellow middle lines (dashed/solid), rare white outer; **many roads unpaved**
**Wooden stilt houses** (vertical planks; dark-red/light-blue/ochre walls w/ bright-blue trim; double/triple roofs)
Majority **Buddhist** (ornate temples); **Angkor/Anchor** beer ads; blue **CPP** party signs (often 3 politicians)
Speed signs say **"MAXIMUM"** (US = "SPEED LIMIT"); km/h, higher numbers
British English (centre, colour) + metric; less wordy signs than the US
**Diamond/checkerboard T-intersection sign = Canada only** (US uses rectangular)
Usually **single** yellow centre line (US = more double yellow)
Lots of **Gen2** (rural/remote) → if Gen2 in N. America, likely Canada; Gen3 mirror = two peaks (US single)
Canada Post **red** logo; communal mailboxes (rare in US, USPS = blue)
West very **mountainous** (BC, W. Alberta, Yukon); Great Plains prairies flat w/ agriculture in the centre
**Canadian Shield** (rocky outcrops + lakes + boreal forest) across the east
Area codes learnable by province; each province has its own **plate** design + flag
**Bollard**: cylindrical, 2 black bands (white or yellow band between); very common at intersections
Rural roads **numbered sequentially** (Twp Rd / Range Rd) → near-exact location from a sign
Thin, slightly curved black+white **bollard** (esp. Vancouver Island); often **concrete barriers** instead of guardrails
Bollard with a plain **black top** (also Saskatchewan); green+white striped sticker on signposts
**Bollard**: cylindrical with 2 *thick* black bands (Alberta's bands are thinner)
**Diamond** roadside markers; km markers with a white square at the bottom
**French majority**; **"Arrêt"** stop sign = QC only ("Stop/Arrêt" bilingual = New Brunswick)
White **bollards attached to guardrails** (red/green reflector); orange & white striped traffic cones
Alternating pole-top insulators (1L/2R ↔ 2L/1R); **white triangles** on signposts
Guardrail wooden posts with a **pyramid-shaped top**; diamond marker beside fire hydrants
Road lines **all white or all yellow** — all-yellow (parks / snowy areas) is ~unique to Chile in the Americas
Gen3 solid **white car, no antenna** (variant with a black section = unique to Chile); Gen4 blue car
**Square concrete poles, indent on both sides** + pinholes on top (fewer supports than Brazil); wooden poles too
Plates white; **commercial & taxi = orange** (also seen in Ecuador)
**Slots at the top of signposts**; chevrons yellow base w/ black arrows (Argentina = white w/ red)
Bollards Spanish-style (white front / yellow-orange back), fairly rare; **bridges with red-painted fence posts**
Long narrow country → **climate by latitude**: arid desert north · Mediterranean centre · cool wet oceanic south
Road naming: **first letter A→Y from north→south**; area codes memorable & regional
**Atacama** (northern third) = extreme arid desert, just sand & bare rock, ~no vegetation
Santiago = semi-arid dry hills; **Maule greener w/ vineyards**; eucalyptus + dry climate → SW Valparaíso
Radiata (**Concepción**) pines w/ puffy needles → Biobío; **monkey-puzzle** trees → far-E Araucanía; narrow tree-lined roads → Biobío/Araucanía
**Triple yellow lines** common in national parks/reserves; all-yellow on concrete → Magallanes (barren treeless far south)
Mainland = **tripod coverage** only (motion blur, saturated colours), no car (HK & Macau have car coverage)
**Simplified** Chinese characters (Traditional → Taiwan/HK/Macau)
Plates **blue with white text**
Coverage = small spots near east-coast cities (Beijing, Shanghai, Shenzhen, Chengdu, Yangzhou); much is inside museums / ancient cities
Plate 1st character = province indicator (京 Beijing, 沪 Shanghai, 粤 Guangdong…) — learnable
Forbidden City / Palace Museum = central Beijing; Zhoucun = only pinpointable ancient city (straight N–S street)
Museum tells the city: Hubei (Wuhan, palace grey roofs), Suzhou (white/grey angular), Long Museum (Shanghai, boxy concrete)
Plates typically **yellow** (in S. America almost exclusively Colombia)
Gen3 short thick **antenna on a black car** (unique — MX/EC/BR have the antenna but not on black); Gen4 white/silver car
km markers: tall **green "Pr"** version + traditional stone marker (both unique to Colombia)
Concrete **dark-coloured poles** w/ black-and-yellow/orange stripe markings; octagonal poles possible (only CO/MX/EC in Americas)
**Wooden fences** very common; some painted with sharp-cut tops (almost unique to Colombia)
**Taxis pastel yellow** with white plates, city name often printed on the side/roof (Ecuador's are more saturated w/ orange plates)
Coverage mostly **north & west** + Caribbean islands San Andrés / Providencia
**North** = white sandy arid soil, cacti (La Guajira = extreme dryness, low vegetation)
**Llanos** (east of the Andes) = open tropical grasslands & savanna w/ scattered trees, seasonal flooding
Pole markings = short black strip top & bottom + longer **yellow middle** → Antioquia (sometimes Córdoba)
**Pacific coast** = majority Afro-Colombian; sugarcane along the Cauca river (Valle del Cauca)
**Barranquilla** intersections marked by concrete **mojones** (street-name posts); San Andrés = white car, Providencia = grey truck
Gen4 = large white pickup w/ a **front antenna** (Panama's truck is almost always fully blurred; Ecuador's has no antenna)
Plates short white w/ blue text → a **light-blue tint** through the blur
Lush tropical: rolling hills, agricultural lowlands, **peaky volcanoes down the centre**, dense rainforest
Architecture less colonial than Panama: **dark-red sheet-metal / plastic-tile roofs**, pastel concrete, heavily barred windows & fences
Spanish; stop sign says **"ALTO"** (Panama = thicker font; rest of Spanish LatAm = "PARE")
**Red taxis** are standard (Panama uses yellow taxis)
Poles: **long silver insulators**; low crossbar w/ 2-on-1 insulators + support bars; L-bent thin metal crossbars
Utility lamps often have a **blue dot** on top (also PA/CO/BR)
Road signs often have a **cross on the back** (esp. diamond warnings); 'bollard' = a vertical **orange board w/ 3 black circles**
**Imperial beer** ads (black-eagle mascot); circular electricity counters; **pole stickers** province-specific (first digit); yellow pole-paint style varies by province
Plate white — some w/ blue strip left, many fully white (Gen3 mostly no strip); **HR** stickers (Hrvatska) on older cars
Bollard white w/ black rectangle + red/white reflector, **sharp triangle from the top** (Hungary/Lithuania are rounder)
**Croatian** (Slavic, Latin only): **IJE** common where Serbian uses just E
Chevrons **red arrow on yellow or white**; **square B-type guardrails** (few European countries use them)
Gen3 quirk: **side mirrors partially visible** + a small **yellow circular sticker on the mirror** (unique to Croatia); Gen3 mostly **autumn**
Direction signs **yellow bg, black outline, pencil-shaped tip** (like other ex-Yugoslav countries)
Poles cylindrical concrete/wood; wooden ones often have **alternating hooks** (like Latvia)
Exposed **red brick** buildings (more inland); **blue fire hydrants** w/ two diagonal protrusions
Pedestrian sign 5 stripes + belt (Slovenia = no belt); Karlovačko/Ožujsko beer, Konzum/Plodine shops; **no Gen2 car** (only trekker)
NE = flat farmland (like Hungary); hilly N/W of Zagreb; **Adriatic coast** = rocky mountains + islands, beige brick w/ green shutters; **Istria** = red soil + Italian bilingual signs
**Zagreb** big-city feel + blue trams; **Dubrovnik** medieval walls (far south); Rijeka blue overhead traffic-light poles; cypress trees near the southern tip
Bollards: **orange double reflector front / single white back** (only Czechia + Slovakia); rare blue/red variants
Special **extra-wide B-type guardrail** used only by Czechia & Slovakia
Wide round concrete poles, often attached in **pairs**; trident / upside-down-triangle poletops
Chevrons **white w/ red arrows**; temporary **red-and-white striped signposts** common
Stripy **gas/water poles** (snow-pole-like) often next to the road
**Hiking markers** (one colour strip between two white) common (also SK/PL/HU)
Prefab **panel apartment blocks** — well-maintained, vibrant colours/patterns; **diamond-tile roofs**
Gen3 antenna w/ **grey tape** on top (also HU/RO/BG); red, black & white Gen4 cars; rare Gen2 (Slovakia has none)
**Czech** distinct letters **Ř Ě Ů** (Slovak: Ľ Ô Ä Ŕ Ĺ); street = **ulice** (not ulica)
Bright **red street signs** (wall or post) + blue/red house-number signs; km markers have a **yellow outline, no middle line**
Black **bridge-end signs** with the road number on the left (Gen4)
Landscape = **rolling hills** + altitude changes, few tall mountains (Slovakia = more mountains + flat areas); grey/snowy winter Gen3
Road number **first digit 1–6 = region**; roads 7/8/9 all start in **Prague** heading N/NW
Long white plate + blue strip; commercial = **yellow**; **"Parrot" plates** (yellow section on the left) are unique to Denmark
Generally **flat** with wide open fields; spacious suburbs, denser urban cores
All-white lines, **double middle common**; **outer lines made of small white squares** — a great Denmark tell
Very unique **direction signs**: low to the ground, **pipe-framed**, red/white or blue/white with the arrow in the background
Street names end **-vej**; blue street sign, white text
Unique **white bollard: yellow reflector + dark-orange stripe** (also a green variant); Belgium's lacks the orange stripe
Danish letters **Æ Ø Å** (Norwegian same; Swedish uses Å Ä Ö instead)
Architecture **red brick**, orange/brown/black tiled roofs; **thatched roofs** rural; timber-frame on historical buildings
Signpost with the **pipe frame bent around the sign** = unique to Denmark
**Square B-type guardrails, no reflectors** — only a few European countries use this
Pedestrian sign **5 stripes**, first & fifth **touch the triangle edge**
Red-and-white **chevrons**, often low to the ground; unique **town-entry sign** (red slash when leaving)
Street-level poles **rare** → mostly big **overhead power lines** near highways; bicycle paths common
**PostNord** postal boxes; gas brands Ingo / OK / Uno-X / YX (each shared with one neighbour)
Road numbering runs **east→west**; roads 2–5 grouped by region; **Bornholm only has 1-roads**
**6 regional bus companies**, each with its own colour scheme + bus-stop sign
**Bornholm**: green/red/white bus stops + green bicycle signs
**Læsø** island: traditional **seaweed (eelgrass) roofs**
**Anholt** island: only **Gen2**, gloomy overcast, sandy dirt on the roadside
Unique car: **two metal bars with thick black stripes** + a white hood at the front (Mongolia's striped car has different landscape)
Poles mostly **square concrete w/ a reverse-'L' top** (in the Americas only DR / Puerto Rico / Uruguay use square poles)
Plates **pale yellow top / white bottom** (New-Jersey-like); no front plate required
Roads often **smooth, notably dark asphalt**; stop sign says **"PARE"**, many signs on **yellow signposts**
Coverage only **Santo Domingo + Santiago de los Caballeros**; proper **mountains to the north = Santiago**
**Santo Domingo** = developed downtown, wide avenues, **"ADN"** on street signs (Distrito Nacional), black-and-purple pole markings
Plates white, a **mix of short & long** (unique in Latin America); commercial = **orange** (solid or an orange top bar)
Gen3 white car w/ a **stubby antenna** (also MX/BR/CO); Gen4 white pickup w/ **black trunk, no antenna** (Panama/Costa Rica have a front antenna)
**Shitcam** — Ecuador is the only Latin American country with it (wide blur, brownish, sometimes a red logo up front)
**Bollards very common** (rare in LatAm): round w/ 2 red stripes, or flat w/ 2 red reflectors on black
**Ladder poles** (many small indents) — ~unique in Latin America (Brazil's waffle poles have only a few)
Concrete roads common; **double guardrails** very common (rare elsewhere in South America)
**Chevron spam** in curves — noticeably more than most Latin American countries
Lots of square white road signs; km markers **green w/ the Ecuadorian highway shield**
**Teal-blue sign backs** (only LatAm country to use them); one/two-way signs w/ text inside the arrow (also DR/PR)
Houses mostly **grey brick + metal roofs** (Peru/Bolivia use red brick); hexagonal-tile pavement in cities
Coverage western (Amazon mostly uncovered; + Galápagos trekker); **Andes** run down the middle, flat E/W; **'high antenna' = northern half**; banana plantations SW
Very sparse — **all Gen3 trekker** coverage (low camera, small blurs, no car shadow) at **7 historic sites** in Cairo & Alexandria
Most buildings are **sandstone**; Arabic script (lines + dots) occasionally on signs
**Giza pyramids** trekker (don't confuse with Memphis's glass pyramid)
**Khafre valley temple** = rows of massive square pillars; **Djoser step pyramid** (stairs + scaffolding) south of Cairo
**Cairo Citadel**: huge clean-cut sandstone walls + Ottoman **Mosque of Muhammad Ali** (grey domes, tall minarets)
**Hanging Church** (Cairo, near Fustat): urban street + church spires w/ crosses, then interior courtyard w/ mosaics & two white spires
**Alexandria — Qaitbay Citadel**: sandstone castle surrounded by thick walls
